back: The son of Mehmed the Conqueror, sultan from 1481. He shifted the empire from constant conquest toward consolidation - strengthening central authority, building infrastructure, and encouraging trade and religious tolerance.

Bayezid II's brother and rival for the throne. After losing, he fled to Europe and became a political hostage of the Pope and Christian monarchs - a bargaining chip that constrained Ottoman foreign policy for years.

front: What happened in 1492 under Bayezid II?
back: As Spain expelled its Jews, Bayezid welcomed thousands of Sephardic refugees into the empire. They settled in cities like Istanbul and Salonica, bringing skills, trade networks, and scholarship that enriched the Ottoman world.

front: Who was Selim I, and why was he called 'the Grim'?
back: Sultan from 1512, known as Yavuz ('the Grim') for the ruthlessness with which he secured power - eliminating rivals including his own brothers. In just eight years he nearly doubled the empire's size.

front: What was the Battle of Chaldiran (1514)?
back: Selim I's decisive victory over the Safavid Empire of Persia. Ottoman firearms and artillery overwhelmed Safavid cavalry, securing eastern Anatolia and beginning a centuries-long Sunni-Shia rivalry between the two empires.

front: Why did Selim I conquer the Mamluk Sultanate?
back: Defeating the Mamluks at Marj Dabiq (1516) and Ridaniya (1517) brought Egypt, Syria, and the holy cities under Ottoman rule. It added vast wealth and let Selim claim the title of Caliph of Islam.

front: What did claiming the Caliphate mean for the Ottoman sultan?
back: By controlling Mecca and Medina, the sultan became the recognized religious and political leader of the entire Sunni Muslim world - not just a powerful king, but the guardian of Islam itself.

Sultan from 1520 to 1566, whose reign was the golden age of the Ottoman Empire. Called 'the Magnificent' in Europe and 'Kanuni' (the Lawgiver) at home, he combined vast conquests with legal reform and cultural brilliance.

front: What was the significance of the Battle of Mohacs (1526)?
back: Suleiman's army destroyed the Hungarian forces in a single afternoon, effectively ending Hungarian independence and bringing much of central Europe under Ottoman control.

front: Why was the 1529 Siege of Vienna important even though it failed?
back: It marked the farthest reach of Ottoman expansion into Europe. The sight of an Ottoman army at Vienna's walls shocked European courts and confirmed the empire as the continent's most formidable military power.

Suleiman's brilliant admiral, who won Ottoman supremacy in the Mediterranean. Under him Ottoman fleets dominated trade routes and extended the empire's reach along the North African coast from Algiers to the Levant.

A former slave who broke tradition by becoming Suleiman's legal wife and trusted adviser. She wielded real political influence and funded major charitable works, opening the door to the era of powerful imperial women.

front: What was the Battle of Lepanto (1571)?
back: A massive naval battle off Greece where the Holy League (Spain, Venice, the papacy) under Don John of Austria destroyed the Ottoman fleet. It broke the myth of Ottoman naval invincibility.

front: How did the Ottomans respond to the defeat at Lepanto?
back: They rebuilt an entire new fleet within a single year, demonstrating the empire's enormous resources and organization. The defeat was a shock to Ottoman prestige but not yet a true decline.

A period of over a century when imperial women - especially the Valide Sultan (mother of the reigning sultan) - held major political power. It began with Hurrem and peaked with Kosem Sultan, who effectively ruled across several reigns.
